source: Body fat percentage visual reference guide PTC.pdf
strategy: agentic chunking via Gemini (headline + summary + original text)

pages.text:
1

BODY FAT PERCENTAGE
VISUAL REFERENCE GUIDE

2
This guide can be used to estimate someone's body fat percentage by eye. All of the
photos in this guide were taken within a few days of having done a high-quality body
composition measurement, e.g. hydrostatic weighing, DXA, InBody, full BIA, underwater
weighing. Most are from clients of Menno. A few publicly posted photos from the
internet are included as well with their names.

As a reference, the following table shows the body composition of the average, healthy
American individual. They are not representative of an "ideal" body composition.
Essential fat is fat associated with bone marrow, the central nervous system, viscera
(internal organs), and cell membranes, and in females, essential fat also includes fat in
mammary glands and the pelvic region. Note that body composition is influenced not
only by gender but also by i.a. age, race, heredity and stature.

3

As an additional reference, you can use the following table from the American Council
on Exercise to classify individuals in terms of leanness. It's good to keep these objective
references in mind next time you hear someone say they're at 2% body fat.
headings: references in mind next time you hear someone say they’re at 2% body fat.

pages.text:
5
Men

4.1% (DXA): Wesley Vissers
Note the grainy appearance of the whole body and the deep muscle striations.

6
6.2% (DXA): Alberto Nuñez
Note the extreme vascularity, striated glutes and Christmas tree lower back definition.
This is pretty much as lean as it gets, so it's a good example that getting below ~5%
body fat is generally only possible with extreme levels of muscle mass.

8
6.3 - 16.7% (DXA)
Here's a selection of DXA scans and corresponding photos of Sigvar Garfors during his
contest prep with Menno Henselmans (in reverse order obviously).

6.3%
Note the start of glute striations and clear separation of the heads of the quads.

10.6%
Note the presence of muscle striations in multiple muscles in his back and the caved in
abdominal structure before being at a single digit body fat percentage.

9

16.7%
Note how a muscular individual at 16.7% body fat still has visible ab definition - one
could say a sixpack when flexed in good lighting (which this isn't at all).

10
8% (DXA)
This is probably an overestimation, as he's very dry.

8% (InBody)

12
8.8% (Hydrostatic weighing): James Krieger
DXA showed a several percent higher fat percentage a few weeks back, so the true
body fat percentage may well be higher, especially since the lower abdominals and
back are still covered by fat.

13
9.6 vs. 10.6% (DXA)
Note how it's hard to tell the difference of 1% change in body fatness with a change in
lighting.

14
9.6 vs. 16.4% (DXA)
Note the lean shoulders and abs without flexing when below 10%. At 16% there is still
faint ab definition.

16
10.2% (DXA)
Note how the abs are visible without flexing, there's separation in his delts and he
carries considerable muscle mass.

17
10.3% (InBody): Menno Henselmans offseason shape

18
11.2 vs. 11.2 vs. 10.2% (DXA): Bodybuilder
From left to right, he first lean bulked while staying at the exact same fat percentage.
Due to being considerably more bloated in the middle photo, he appears much fatter.
It's difficult to learn to distinguish between water retention and fat.

In the right photo he's a little leaner, but as you can see it's very difficult to be accurate
to a single percent visually. It mostly shows in his lower back.

20
11.6 vs 12.3% (DXA): Brad Pilon
It's hard to tell the difference with the change in lighting.

11.9% (BodPod)

21
11.9% vs. 19% (DXA)
Worse lighting when leaner makes it somewhat hard to tell the difference, but look at
the more capped delts, the obliques and the vascularity.

22
12.1 vs. 7.9% (DXA)
Before at 12.1% (above) and after photoshoot prep at 7.9% (below). Note how at 12%
he already has a full sixpack when flexed and when he's 'ripped', he's really still 8%. It
is quite hard to get to stage condition without a lot of muscle mass. Also note how
professional photos can make you look signficantly leaner.

24
10% to 12.1% (BodPod)
These photos are from the bulk phase of one of Menno's clients. He went from 192 lb at
10% body fat to 216 lb at 12.1% body fat over about half a year. This progression
illustrates how adding a substantial amount of muscle mass can make you look leaner
even while gaining several pounds of fat.

26
12.9% (DXA) / 12.3% (InBody)

13% (DXA)
These were posted publicly, so we can't verify this fat percentage with certainty, but it
looks accurate given that he's muscular enough and bloated.

27
13% (DXA & Skulpt)

28
13.7% (DXA)
Don't be fooled by his stocky physique. The ab separation is clearly indicative of being
sub 15%.

14% - 10.3% (DXA)
Note how his vascularity and abdominals improve as he leans out (and gained muscle).

30
16.1% (DXA)
Note how his loose skin makes him look significantly fatter than he really is.

16.7% (DXA)

31
17.4% (DXA)

17.6%: skinny vs. trained individual (DXAs)

32
17.8% (DXA)
This is a telling example of where many men think they're 'a bit over 10%' when in
reality they're far closer to being overweight. Abdominal definition normally occurs well
before a man is at 10% body fat, assuming a decent amount of muscle mass.

33
19% (DXA)
This is a good example of the skinny-fat look.

34
19% (DXA): Mike Cernovich

36
21.8% (DXA): Overweight from here on

37
22% to 16.9% (DXA): Bret Contreras

40
24.8% (DXA)
Typical skinny fat example.

41
25.5% (DXA)
This is a good example of large abdominal fat storage.

42
26.6% (DXA): Obese from here on

43
27.4% (DXA)
Typical skinny fat example.

28.2% (hydrostatic weighing)

48
40% to 33.8% (DXA)
Note the loose skin after weight loss.

50
Women

12.8% (DXA): Figure competitor
Note the ab vascularity, grainy skin and muscle striations, including the start of glute
striations.

51
14%? (InBody)
InBody scans typically underestimate the body fat percentage of strength trainees at
lower fat levels, so Menno's client is probably more like 18% here in reality.

52
17.4% & 17% (DXA): Bikini contest shape

53
18% (BodPod): Female physique competitor (off-season)
Note how having a lot of muscle drives down your body fat percentage, although this
may be an underestimation.

54
20.2% vs. 16.8% (DXA)
Note how at over 20% body fat she already has visible abdominal definition without
flexing when in favorable lighting. Much of the fat loss was in her lower posterior chain.

56
This series clearly shows how significantly different the body fat distribution of men and
women can be. The glute-ham area stores a very large amount of fat for many women
and this area is notoriously difficult to get lean.

Also note how unfavorable lighting can mask even competition level conditioning (13%
BF) in women without a competitive physique athlete level of muscle mass. Below is a
comparison shot with more favorable lighting and after a water cut (dehydration) at
~13% body fat.

58
21.5% (InBody)

59

20% vs. 18% (hydrostatic weighing)

62
22.5% (InBody)
This is probably a slight underestimation.

64
25% (BIA)
This is a typical feminine body fat storage pattern with most fat stored on the lower body
and hips.

65
26% (DXA)
This is a typical 'love handle' body fat storage pattern for an otherwise quite lean
woman.

26% (DXA)

67
36.7% (DXA): Overweight

44% (DXA): Obese
